name: Young-trained donor FMT improved old mouse cognition and synaptic plasticity
summary: Young trained donor FMT supports gut-brain-axis functional benefit in aged mice, with exercise-associated donor status as an important boundary.
statement: In middle-aged and aged mice, FMT from young trained donors improved recognition and spatial memory, preserved hippocampal LTP, reduced neuroinflammation, increased synaptic plasticity modulators, reduced ileal and colon permeability, shifted SCFAs, and altered microbial community composition compared with age-matched or young sedentary donor controls.
population_or_model: Twelve-month-old and 18-month-old male mice receiving FMT from young trained, young sedentary, or age-matched donors
time_horizon: Post-FMT cognitive, electrophysiology, gut barrier, cytokine, SCFA, and microbiome assessment
quantitative_note: The abstract reports significant recognition and spatial memory enhancement in young-trained FMT groups but does not provide numeric effect sizes.
caveats: The donor phenotype combines youth and exercise training, so the result may not generalize to unscreened young donors.
Mouse cognition and LTP are not durable human cognitive or dementia outcomes.
The study informs donor-selection hypotheses more than a defined clinical intervention.
